old_claim.note: Industrial Safety Regulation provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct industrial_safety_regulation dependency remains because the 1869 Westinghouse air-brake invention is source-backed as a compressed-air railroad mechanism, while regulation is a later adoption and mandate context.

source_shape.source_locator: The Library of Congress states the Westinghouse Air Brake Company was organized in 1869 to manufacture George Westinghouse's air brakes and describes the first air brake as a railroad-safety invention.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source grounds the node in Westinghouse's 1869 air-brake invention and manufacturing company rather than in an industrial safety regulation regime.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source explains the technology's safety benefit but does not make regulation a prerequisite for the 1869 mechanism; compressed-air operation and railroads cover the direct technical dependencies.

ontology_before: Railway Air Brakes depended on Industrial Safety Regulation, implying occupational safety law enabled the initial 1869 air-brake technology.
ontology_after: Railway Air Brakes depends on railroads and compressed-air power; later safety mandates are deployment context rather than first-known invention prerequisites.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: railway_air_brakes no longer directly depends on industrial_safety_regulation.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to regulatory adoption of automatic train brakes rather than the 1869 Westinghouse compressed-air brake.
A stronger source showed Westinghouse's 1869 air-brake invention required industrial safety regulation to exist first.
The industrial_safety_regulation node were narrowed to a specific railroad air-brake mandate predating the invention.
short_reason: Safety regulation explains later adoption pressure, not the 1869 air-brake mechanism.
